title: Fraud / Abuse Detection — System Design
description: Design a risk engine that decides, in real time, whether an action (payment, login, signup) is fraudulent or abusive. The themes are fast inline decisioning (rules + ML), near-real-time velocity/graph features, a human-review loop, and a feedback loop that turns confirmed outcomes into better models.

nodes.label: Calling Service
nodes.description: Asks 'is this action risky?' inline (e.g. checkout, login, signup).

nodes.label: Risk Decision API
nodes.description: Synchronous decision: allow / block / challenge / review.

nodes.label: Rules Engine
nodes.description: Evaluates deterministic rules and velocity checks fast.

nodes.label: ML Scoring Model
nodes.description: Returns a risk score from features.

nodes.label: Event Stream
nodes.description: All actions/events; drives feature computation and offline analysis.

nodes.label: Entity / Link Graph
nodes.description: Links users/devices/cards/IPs to detect rings and shared identity.

nodes.label: Label / Outcome Store
nodes.description: Confirmed fraud/legit labels (incl. chargebacks) for training.

nodes.label: Offline Trainer
nodes.description: Retrains the model from labeled outcomes.

functional: Score an action's risk synchronously and return a decision (allow/block/challenge/review).
Combine deterministic rules and an ML risk model.
Use velocity and reputation features (this user/device/IP recently).
Detect coordinated abuse (fraud rings, sockpuppets) via entity links.
Route ambiguous cases to human review and learn from labeled outcomes.
nonFunctional: Low decision latency — it's inline on a user action (tens of ms).
High recall on fraud without too many false positives (block real users).
Adapt quickly: attackers change tactics, so rules/models must update fast.
Resilient: a scoring outage degrades per a per-action fallback policy (not one universal 'fail open'), never an outage of the host action.
Privacy & abuse-resistant: tokenized identifiers, retention limits by record type, analyst RBAC, internal-only reason codes, and defenses against API probing and event/label poisoning.

capacity.label: Decision QPS
capacity.note: Assume ~5k risk decisions/sec at peak (logins + payments + signups), ~1.5k average. High-value payments are ~10% of that; the latency budget and fleet are sized to peak.

capacity.label: Event write amplification
capacity.note: Each decision emits the action event plus follow-up outcome events (auth result, challenge result, settlement) -> ~10-20k events/sec into the stream. Sizes stream partitions and aggregator parallelism.

capacity.label: Feature reads / score
capacity.note: Each score reads counters/reputation for several entities (user, device, IP, card) -> ~50-150k feature reads/sec. Must come from an in-memory store (<5ms) to fit the budget.

capacity.label: Feature update rate
capacity.note: The aggregator writes updated counters per event. Hot keys (shared IP, popular merchant, attack device) concentrate writes -> partition by entity and shard hot keys.

capacity.label: Decision latency
capacity.note: Inline on checkout. Rough budget: rules ~5ms, feature reads ~10ms, model ~15ms, graph features (precomputed) ~5ms, overhead ~15ms. Graph traversal is never done inline.

capacity.label: Feature freshness
capacity.note: Velocity counters must reflect just-happened activity; stream->aggregate->store lag is targeted at 1-3s.

capacity.label: Graph edge growth
capacity.note: Each event may add user-device/IP/card links; the graph is large and append-heavy. Edge features are materialized offline, not traversed inline.

capacity.label: Review queue arrival
capacity.note: ~50-150 cases/sec at peak go to async review. Analyst throughput (cases/analyst/hour) bounds the challenge/review band width and the backlog SLA.

capacity.label: Label lag
capacity.note: Analyst labels arrive in minutes-hours; chargebacks land 1-6 weeks later. Point-in-time joins must rejoin to decision-time feature snapshots.

capacity.label: Audit retention
capacity.note: Each decision stores score, reasons, versions, and entity refs. ~5k/sec x 1-2KB -> ~0.5-1 TB/day; retained 12-24 months for disputes/regulators -> compress and tier to cold storage.

api.description: Score an action and return a decision synchronously. The caller supplies a stable actionId and an idempotencyKey so retries replay the original decision; same key + same payload returns the original decision, same key + different payload is rejected (409).

Report an action/outcome event (feeds features and offline analysis). dedupeKey makes ingestion idempotent so retried/replayed events don't double-count velocity counters; eventTime (when it happened) is separate from server ingestTime (when it arrived) for late/out-of-order handling. This is also how a caller closes a workflow: lifecycle outcomes carry the decisionId and a type of challenge_passed / challenge_failed / review_resolved / payment_reversed / chargeback so the engine can complete the challenge_attempt or review_case, emit the matching label, and refresh velocity/reputation features.

Record a confirmed outcome (fraud/legit, chargeback) for training. Joins to the exact decision via decisionId and to its decision-time feature snapshot; source carries a trust level so the trainer can weight/quarantine low-trust labels.

Observe an async workflow opened by a decision. A challenge decision returns a challengeId and a review decision returns a reviewCaseId; both are queryable here so the caller knows whether to hold the action, when a challenge expires, and the final outcome. Terminal resolutions (challenge_passed/failed, review fraud/legit/reversed/refunded) emit the matching label and, where relevant, drive a reversal/refund. Callers that prefer push can register a webhook instead of polling.

dataModel.name: decisions
dataModel.note: Audit record of each decision + the signals behind it. The join anchor for events, labels, and review cases.

dataModel.name: events
dataModel.note: Append-only log of action/outcome events; source of all features.

dataModel.name: feature_snapshots
dataModel.note: Decision-time feature values referenced by each decision; enables point-in-time training joins.

dataModel.name: graph_edges
dataModel.note: Entity link graph; edge features are materialized offline, never traversed inline.

dataModel.name: review_cases
dataModel.note: Lifecycle of a flagged case; state machine new -> assigned -> resolved.

fields.name: evidence_ref
fields.type: snapshot of signals + graph at flag time

dataModel.name: challenge_attempts
dataModel.note: Outcome record of an inline step-up challenge.

dataModel.name: rulesets / decision_policies / model_versions
dataModel.note: Versioned governance for what was in effect at decision time and how it rolled out.

dataModel.name: labels
dataModel.note: Confirmed outcomes for supervised training; joins back to the exact decision + feature snapshot.

tradeoff: A small stateless risk service behind Envoy/Kong keeps p99 latency and caller-specific behavior under your control.
A managed gateway/WAF gives throttling, auth integration, TLS termination, and edge request logging with less custom middleware.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed edge with per-caller quotas and WAF rules removes much of the custom API probing and rate-limit code from the RiskAPI fleet.

tradeoff: A rules engine or rules-as-code flow keeps decisions explainable and lets analysts ship emergency rules quickly.
Remote configuration is simpler for thresholds and tier bands, but weaker for complex rule authoring and approval workflows.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Versioned policy/config storage removes hard-coded thresholds and many emergency application deploys.

tradeoff: In-memory stores fit the sub-5ms feature-read budget but need careful persistence and failover choices.
Wide-column or durable KV stores handle high write rates and longer retention, at higher read latency and cost.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed Redis/KV store with TTL, conditional writes, and replication removes much of the custom shard failover and expiry machinery.

technologyChoices.concern: Event stream and idempotent ingestion log
tradeoff: A durable log is the right default for replay, feature recompute, graph updates, and multiple downstream consumers.
A queue is simpler for isolated async work, but weaker for long replay and point-in-time backfills.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed stream with retention, lag metrics, and native dead-letter handling removes most broker operations from the feature pipeline.

tradeoff: Flink-style processors give event-time windows, watermarks, checkpoints, and exactly-once sinks for velocity counters.
Serverless or batch-oriented processors reduce operations, but make hot-key mitigation and second-fresh updates harder to control.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed streaming with checkpoints and autoscaling removes many custom watermark, retry, and consumer-rebalance jobs.

tradeoff: Graph-native storage is useful for analyst exploration and offline ring mining.
For the inline path, wide-column/document stores plus precomputed ring features are usually safer than live traversal.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Materializing graph features into the online feature store makes inline graph traversal unnecessary.

technologyChoices.concern: Model serving and bounded risk scoring
tradeoff: Self-managed model serving gives tight control over p99 latency, feature fetches, and canary routing.
Managed ML endpoints simplify packaging, registry integration, autoscaling, and rollback, but add provider-specific latency and deployment semantics.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed endpoint with registry, canary, and rollback support removes much of the bespoke model deployment service.

tradeoff: A workflow engine is useful when challenge/review states, retries, holds, expirations, and appeals become first-class product flows.
A simple queue plus database state is cheaper at first, but pushes leases, idempotency, and retries into application code.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed queue/workflow services remove much of the custom lease, retry, dead-letter, and state-transition plumbing.

technologyChoices.concern: Label store, training pipeline, and point-in-time joins
tradeoff: Point-in-time feature joins prevent training leakage and keep delayed chargebacks tied to decision-time snapshots.
Managed warehouses and ML platforms are usually cheaper for long label lag than keeping all training state in operational databases.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed ML/lakehouse stack removes much of the ad hoc feature export, training orchestration, and artifact publishing code.

technologyChoices.concern: Decision audit, evidence search, and retention tiers
tradeoff: Hot decision records need low-latency lookup for retries, disputes, and case evidence.
Cold append-only storage is the economical place for 12-24 months of compressed audit records and training backfills.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Object storage with lifecycle tiering removes the need to keep every decision and event in the hot serving database.

technologyChoices.concern: Versioned rollout and governance for rules, models, and policies
tradeoff: Rules, thresholds, feature definitions, and models need immutable versions because every decision can be disputed later.
Feature flags and canaries enable shadow/champion-challenger rollout, but require rollback discipline and complete audit trails.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A shared registry/config plane removes hand-built version tables for many rollout and approval workflows.

tradeoff: The platform must alert on p99 score latency, feature age, stream lag, fallback rate, review backlog, label drift, and poisoning probes.
Managed telemetry reduces ingestion and storage operations, while self-hosted stacks provide more control over retention and sensitive signal redaction.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed telemetry with alert rules removes custom health scripts, but not domain-specific fraud dashboards and drift monitors.

patterns.name: Inline rules + ML decisioning
patterns.what: Combine fast deterministic rules with an ML score to a single decision synchronously.
patterns.whenToUse: Real-time risk decisions on a user action.

patterns.name: Streaming velocity features
patterns.what: Compute counters (events per entity per window) in near-real-time from the event stream.
patterns.whenToUse: Catching bursts/abuse that need fresh aggregates.

patterns.name: Entity link graph
patterns.what: Link users/devices/cards/IPs to surface rings and shared-identity abuse.
patterns.whenToUse: Coordinated fraud that single-entity signals miss.

patterns.name: Sync-vs-async decisioning
patterns.what: Block obvious fraud inline; flag ambiguous cases for async review or step-up challenge.
patterns.whenToUse: Balancing latency, recall, and false positives.
patterns.group: Sync vs Async: Block, Challenge, Review

patterns.name: Human-in-the-loop labeling
patterns.what: Route ambiguous cases to analysts whose labels become training data.
patterns.whenToUse: When automated confidence is low and labels are scarce.
patterns.group: Sync vs Async: Block, Challenge, Review

patterns.name: Feedback loop / retraining
patterns.what: Turn confirmed outcomes (chargebacks, reviews) into labels to retrain models.
patterns.group: The Feedback Loop: Labels and Retraining

steps.title: 1. Naive: A Few Static If-Rules Inline in the App (the baseline)
description: Start with the simplest thing that works. The calling service has a handful of hard-coded checks inline: is this card on a blocklist? Is the amount over $X? Is the IP in a banned country? If any fires, block; otherwise allow.

This is cheap, fast, and explainable — and it exposes the problems the rest of the design exists to solve. Static rules only catch fraud you already wrote a rule for: they miss fuzzy, novel, and coordinated fraud (a ring of fresh accounts each individually clean, a fraudster just under the amount threshold). They are crude — a single $X cutoff blocks legitimate big spenders (false positives) while letting smart fraud through. And because the rules are hard-coded in the app, adapting to a new attack means a code change and deploy, far too slow against an adversary that changes tactics daily.

Even this baseline grows obligations the moment it becomes a shared risk service rather than inline code: callers will retry it, so the same action scored twice must return the same decision (idempotency, not a fresh roll), and every decision needs an audit record (what fired, when, for whom) for disputes and debugging. That's why the decision API later carries an actionId, an idempotencyKey, and a durable decisionId — the baseline already needs them.

So the baseline motivates everything that follows: add an ML model that scores fuzzy risk from fresh behavioral features, link entities to catch rings, route ambiguous cases to humans, and feed confirmed outcomes back to keep learning.

view.caption: The calling service asks the risk decision API synchronously, which evaluates a handful of static rules in the rules engine.

steps.decisionPrompt: If your only checks are a blocklist and 'amount > $X', how do you catch a ring of fresh accounts that each look clean, and how fast can you react to a new attack?
whyNow: Establishing the static-rules baseline first makes it obvious why fuzzy ML scoring, fresh features, graph signals, and a feedback loop are needed — every later step removes a specific blind spot from here.

recap.after: A few hard-coded if-rules (blocklist, amount cap) decide allow/block inline in the app.
recap.newRisk: Static rules miss fuzzy and coordinated fraud, over-block legit users, and can't adapt without a redeploy.

traps.trap: Relying only on a few static if-rules baked into the application.
traps.why: They catch only known patterns, cause false positives with crude cutoffs, and can't adapt to a fast-moving adversary.
traps.instead: Combine rules with an ML score over fresh features, add graph signals, and close a feedback loop that retrains.

traps.trap: Treating each retried request as a brand-new action with no record of the decision.
traps.why: Inline callers retry; without a stable action id / idempotency key the same attempt looks like several, double-counts velocity, and leaves no auditable record of why it was blocked.
traps.instead: Give every action a stable id + idempotency key from the start and persist an audit record of each decision — even the naive baseline should be replay-safe and explainable.

steps.title: 2. Inline Decisioning: Rules + ML
description: Risk is asked inline on a user action (checkout, login), so it must answer in tens of ms with a decision: allow, block, challenge (step-up auth), or send to review. The risk API combines two engines.

A rules engine handles fast, deterministic checks (blocklists, hard velocity limits, impossible-travel) — cheap, explainable, and instantly updatable. An ML model returns a risk score from features for the fuzzy cases rules can't capture.

Combine them: hard rules can short-circuit (block known-bad immediately); otherwise the score plus rule signals map to a decision with thresholds. The model depends on fresh behavioral features — assume they exist for now; Step 3 designs how we keep them fresh.

Every decision persists an auditable record: a decisionId, the reasons, and the exact versions in effect — ruleset version, model version, and threshold/policy version — plus internal reason codes kept separate from the vague message shown to the user. Those versions are what later let a chargeback or dispute be replayed against exactly what the engine saw.

view.caption: The calling service calls the risk API inline, which combines the rules engine with a risk score from the ML scoring model.

options.name: Rules engine + ML model combined (default)
options.description: The risk API runs a deterministic rules engine that can short-circuit known-bad, and otherwise blends a model risk score with rule signals into an allow/block/challenge/review decision.
pros: Hard rules short-circuit known-bad instantly (blocklists, velocity limits) — cheap and explainable.
The ML model scores the fuzzy, novel cases that no static rule captures.
New attacks can be blocked the moment a rule is added, while the model handles the long tail.
cons: Two engines to build, tune, and keep consistent (e.g. rules that contradict the model).
Combining a rule verdict with a model score needs a clear policy for who wins.
More moving parts inside the tens-of-ms inline budget.

view.caption: The risk API runs the rules engine and blends its signals with the ML model's risk score into one decision.

options.name: Rules engine only
options.description: Every inline decision comes from deterministic checks alone — blocklists, velocity limits, and impossible-travel conditions — with no model on the path.
pros: Every decision is fully explainable and auditable — critical for disputes and regulators.
Trivially fast and cheap; no model serving or feature store on the inline path.
Analysts can add or change a rule and see the effect immediately.
cons: Only catches fraud you already wrote a rule for — misses fuzzy and novel patterns.
Fraudsters probe thresholds and stay just under the limits.
Rule sets grow into an unmaintainable thicket of overlapping conditions.

view.caption: The risk API decides purely from the rules engine's deterministic checks, with no model on the path.

options.name: ML model only
options.description: A single ML model scores each action from its features and the score maps to a decision, with no separate rules engine in the loop.
pros: Captures subtle, correlated, and novel fraud that hand-written rules can't express.
A single scoring path is conceptually simpler than maintaining two engines.
Improves automatically as it retrains on new labeled outcomes.
cons: No instant lever for a brand-new attack — you must retrain and redeploy.
Scores are hard to explain, which hurts disputes and analyst trust.
A model outage leaves no fast deterministic fallback to keep checkout safe.

view.caption: The risk API derives the decision solely from the ML scoring model, with no separate rules engine.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why combine a rules engine with an ML model instead of using only one?

concepts.definition: Deterministic rules for known patterns plus an ML score for fuzzy risk, combined into one decision.
concepts.whyItMatters: Rules give instant, explainable control; ML generalizes — together they cover more with fewer gaps.
concepts.example: Rule blocks a known-bad card instantly; model flags a subtle anomaly.

concepts.definition: A stable action id + idempotency key replays the original decision on retry; the audit record pins the ruleset/model/policy versions in effect.
concepts.whyItMatters: Retries don't double-count or create conflicting decisions, and a later dispute can be replayed against exactly what the engine saw.
concepts.example: Same idempotencyKey -> original decision returned; record shows policy p_2026_06, model m_42.

whyNow: The inline decision is the product; framing it as rules + ML with an explainable output is the core architecture.

recap.newRisk: The model is only as good as its features — and fraud needs fresh, behavioral features.

flows.name: Inline decision: rule short-circuit or ML score
flows.note: A hard rule (known-bad) blocks immediately; otherwise the model scores from fresh features and the score maps to allow/challenge/review.

steps.title: 3. Streaming Velocity Features
description: Most fraud signal is behavioral and recent: 'this card tried 20 merchants in 5 minutes', 'this IP created 100 accounts today'. These velocity/aggregate features must be fresh (seconds), so compute them in near-real-time.

Every action emits an event to a stream; a streaming aggregator maintains per-entity counters (per user/device/IP/card, per window) and reputation, writing them to a low-latency feature store the model/rules read at decision time. At ~10-20k events/sec this is the work-driving path: partition the stream by entity type + entity hash (so order is preserved per entity), and split known hot keys (a shared IP, a popular merchant, an attack device) into sub-partitions so one entity can't bottleneck a partition. Concretely, ~10-20k events/sec at a few thousand events/sec per consumer is on the order of dozens of partitions with consumer parallelism to match, plus headroom for replay/backfill after an outage.

Size the feature store from the read path, not the write path: ~50-150k reads/sec against ~10-30 features/score means the hot set must be in memory and replicated. Shard by entity key and add read replicas per shard so hot entities (a shared IP, a viral merchant) don't hotspot a single node; replicas also absorb the read fan-out and give failover. This is why graph traversal is materialized out — it would multiply this read load by the neighborhood size.

Counters are only trustworthy if ingestion is clean. Events carry an eventId + dedupeKey so retried/replayed events are dropped (otherwise a single retried login inflates velocity and poisons the score), and an eventTime separate from ingestTime so the aggregator can window on when things happened and tolerate late/out-of-order arrivals (watermarks, bounded lateness) rather than miscounting a burst.

Online/offline parity matters: the same feature definitions used at scoring must be used in training, or the model sees different inputs than it learned on. The exact values served are captured in a decision-time feature snapshot so later labels can be joined point-in-time.

view.caption: Actions emit events to the event stream, which the streaming aggregator consumes to update the feature store that both the rules engine and the model read.

options.name: Streaming aggregator into a low-latency feature store (default)
options.description: Every action emits an event; a stateful streaming aggregator maintains per-entity windowed counters in a low-latency feature store that rules and the model read at decision time.
pros: Velocity features (20 merchants in 5 minutes) are fresh within seconds, catching fast-moving fraud.
The same aggregation code feeds both online serving and offline training, giving online/offline parity.
The inline model reads precomputed counters, so scoring stays within the tens-of-ms budget.
cons: A streaming pipeline (event log + stateful aggregator + feature store) is real operational complexity.
Late or out-of-order events can momentarily skew counters.
Stateful windowed aggregation must be made fault-tolerant and replayable.

view.caption: Events flow from the caller through the event stream into a streaming aggregator that maintains windowed counters in the feature store for the model.

options.name: Batch-computed features (hourly/daily)
options.description: Velocity and reputation features are recomputed by periodic batch jobs over the event log and loaded into the feature store on a fixed schedule.
pros: Simple batch jobs over the event log — no stateful streaming machinery to operate.
Cheap to recompute and easy to reason about for training/serving parity.
Fine for slow-moving signals like long-term reputation or lifetime spend.
cons: Hours-stale counters miss exactly the burst fraud (rapid-fire attempts) that matters most.
A card cycling 20 merchants in 5 minutes looks clean until the next batch lands.
Adversaries exploit the lag window between batches.

view.caption: A periodic batch job scans the event stream and writes aggregated features into the feature store that the model reads.

options.name: On-demand feature computation at decision time
options.description: Counters are computed live per request by scanning recent events from the event log the moment a decision is needed, with no precomputed feature store.
pros: Always exactly fresh — counts are computed from the source the instant a decision is made.
No separate feature store to keep in sync or worry about staleness.
Fewer pipelines to operate.
cons: Scanning recent events per request is far too slow for a tens-of-ms inline budget.
Heavy read load on the event store for every checkout and login.
Doesn't scale to many entities and windows per decision.

view.caption: The risk API computes counters live by scanning recent events from the event stream before calling the model, with no feature store.

steps.decisionPrompt: How does the model know this card was used at 20 merchants in the last 5 minutes, within a 20ms budget?

concepts.definition: Near-real-time per-entity counters/aggregates over recent windows.
concepts.whyItMatters: Catches bursty/coordinated abuse that static features miss; must be fresh and fast to read.
concepts.example: payments_last_5m for a card, served from the feature store in <5ms.

concepts.definition: Window on when an event happened (with dedupe + bounded lateness), and capture the exact feature values served at decision time.
concepts.whyItMatters: Deduped event-time counters resist replay/poisoning, and snapshots let late labels be joined point-in-time without leakage.
concepts.example: Late login windowed by eventTime; decision dec_91's snapshot stores the counters it scored on.

whyNow: Fresh behavioral features are what make fraud detection work; the stream->aggregate->feature-store path is the standard mechanism.

traps.trap: Counting raw events without a dedupe key.
traps.why: Inline callers retry; a single replayed action inflates velocity counters and can flip the score — and is an easy poisoning vector for attackers who replay benign-looking events.
traps.instead: Deduplicate on an event/dedupe key at ingest, and window on event time (not ingest time) with bounded lateness.

failureDrills.scenario: An action is retried and emits the same event twice (or an attacker replays events).
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The second event is deduped on its dedupe key, so counters are unaffected and the score isn't poisoned.
failureDrills.mitigation: Idempotent ingest (dedupe key) + event-time windowing with watermarks for late/out-of-order events.

recap.after: Fresh, dedup'd velocity/reputation features feeding rules + model.
recap.newRisk: Sophisticated fraud is coordinated across many entities — single-entity features miss rings.

steps.title: 4. Entity Graph for Coordinated Abuse
description: Fraud rings share resources: many 'users' on one device, many cards behind one IP, recycled phone numbers. Single-entity features can't see this; an entity link graph connecting users/devices/cards/IPs/addresses can.

The aggregator maintains these links from events; graph features (cluster size, shared-device count, connection to known-bad entities) feed the model. A new account linked to a known fraud cluster is high-risk even with a clean individual history.

Keep the inline path cheap: do not traverse the graph at decision time. Edges are written offline/near-real-time (~10-20k/sec), and graph features are materialized into the same feature store the model reads in <5ms — the decision reads a precomputed cluster-size/known-bad-distance value, never a live multi-hop query. Edges carry a confidence and first/last-seen so stale links can expire (a device reassigned by an ISP shouldn't link strangers forever), and shared identifiers are tokenized for privacy.

Because the features are materialized, expiry has to actively invalidate them, not just stop traversing. When an edge ages out or its confidence decays, the affected entities' graph features must be recomputed and rewritten, or the feature store keeps serving an inflated cluster size long after the link is gone — a classic stale shared-IP/device false positive. So edge expiry triggers re-materialization for the touched neighborhood (event-driven on expiry, with a periodic sweep as a backstop), the same way new edges do.
Graph signals are powerful for catching organized abuse and bot rings that look individually benign — a key differentiator from per-event scoring.

view.caption: The streaming aggregator consumes events to maintain the entity link graph, whose ring/link features feed the ML scoring model.

steps.decisionPrompt: A brand-new account looks clean individually. How might it still be obviously fraudulent?

concepts.definition: A graph linking shared identifiers (device, IP, card) to reveal clusters/rings.
concepts.whyItMatters: Surfaces coordinated abuse invisible to single-entity features.
concepts.example: New user shares a device with 50 chargeback accounts -> high risk.

whyNow: Graph features catch organized fraud that per-event scoring misses; it's the depth that separates a strong fraud answer.

recap.after: Ring/cluster detection via the entity graph.
recap.newRisk: Not every case is clear-cut — ambiguous ones need a path beyond instant allow/block.

steps.title: 5. Sync vs Async: Block, Challenge, Review
description: Not every decision should be a hard inline allow/block. Block only high-confidence fraud (false positives block real customers); for medium risk, issue a step-up challenge (2FA, ID check) inline; for ambiguous cases, allow-but-flag and send to async human review rather than guessing.

A review queue is not just a queue — each case is a small state machine: created with an evidence snapshot (the signals + graph as they were at flag time), prioritized by risk x value, assigned to an analyst, worked under an SLA (due_at), and resolved (fraud / legit / reversed / refunded). The resolution emits a label and any customer action (hold, refund, clawback); appeals/disputes reopen the case. Analyst access is RBAC-gated and every action is audit-logged.

Make the workflow observable to the caller, not just internal. The decision response carries a challengeId or reviewCaseId; the caller polls GET /v1/risk/cases/{id} (or gets a webhook) to learn whether to hold the action, when a challenge expires, and the final resolution. Completion flows back the other way as an event: challenge_passed / challenge_failed and review_resolved / payment_reversed POST to /v1/events with the decisionId, which closes the challenge_attempt or review_case, emits the matching label, and refreshes features — so the lifecycle is fully traceable end to end.

Review capacity is a hard constraint, not a queue depth. At ~1-3% of decisions, peak review arrival is ~50-150 cases/sec — far beyond what any analyst team can clear (a few cases/analyst/hour means even a large team handles tens of cases/sec at most). So the review band is sized to staffing, not the other way around: prioritize by risk x value and work the top of the queue first, auto-resolve or hold the long tail by policy when the backlog SLA is at risk, and throttle the allow-but-flag band (raise its threshold) when the backlog ages. The decision_policies thresholds are tuned with this analyst budget in view, which is why a threshold tweak that quietly doubles the backlog must be caught.

Outcomes feed training, but only the resolved label and decision-time features become a training label — analyst-only investigative evidence is not leaked into the model or back to the customer. Whether 'allow-but-flag' is even acceptable depends on the action: fine for a low-value payment or login, but high-value payments may need to hold/challenge rather than complete-then-review.
This sync/async split balances latency, recall, and false positives: instant for the obvious, challenge for the uncertain, async review for the genuinely ambiguous.

view.caption: The risk API flags ambiguous charges to the review queue, where an analyst reviews each case and writes the outcome to the label store.

options.name: Three tiers: block / challenge / async review (default)
options.description: Decisions split by confidence: hard-block the obvious fraud, issue an inline step-up challenge in the uncertain middle, and route ambiguous cases to an async analyst review queue.
pros: Hard-blocks only high-confidence fraud, so real customers aren't turned away on a borderline score.
Step-up challenges let a legit user in the uncertain middle prove themselves inline.
Ambiguous cases go to async human review, which both resolves them and generates high-quality labels.
cons: Three thresholds to tune against a business cost matrix — more policy to get right.
Requires a challenge flow (2FA/ID) and a staffed review queue to operate.
Friction from challenges can still cost some legitimate conversions.

view.caption: The risk API routes uncertain cases to the review queue, where an analyst decides and records the outcome in the label store.

options.name: Binary allow/block inline only
options.description: Every transaction gets one inline verdict from a single threshold — allow or block — with no challenge step and no human review queue.
pros: Simplest possible policy — one threshold, one decision, no queues or challenge flow.
Every transaction gets an instant verdict with no human in the loop.
Nothing extra to staff or operate.
cons: Forces a guess on every borderline score: block good customers or let fraud through.
No step-up path for the uncertain middle where most ambiguity lives.
Generates no analyst labels, starving the retraining loop of fresh ground truth.

view.caption: The calling service gets a single inline allow/block verdict from the risk API, with no review queue.

options.name: Allow-but-flag everything to async review
options.description: No transaction is blocked inline; risky ones are flagged and sent to the analyst review queue, where outcomes are decided and reversed after the fact.
pros: Never blocks a customer inline, so legitimate conversions are never lost at checkout.
Every flagged case is examined by an analyst, maximizing label quality.
Decisions can be reversed after the fact (refund, clawback) rather than pre-emptively.
cons: Confirmed fraud is allowed to complete before review, so losses are realized first.
The review queue must absorb a huge volume, which doesn't scale to inline traffic.
No inline protection means high-confidence attacks succeed in real time.

view.caption: The risk API allows but flags every risky charge to the review queue, where an analyst labels outcomes after the fact in the label store.

steps.decisionPrompt: A charge scores 0.6 — not clearly fraud, not clearly legit. What do you do with it?

concepts.definition: Inline block/challenge for confident cases; async review for ambiguous ones.
concepts.whyItMatters: Avoids blocking good users while still catching fraud and gathering labels.
concepts.example: Score>0.9 block; 0.5-0.9 challenge; flagged -> review queue.
concepts.group: Sync vs Async: Block, Challenge, Review

whyNow: The decision-type split is the practical heart of a risk engine; conflating everything into block/allow causes false positives and missed fraud.

recap.newRisk: Attackers adapt; static rules/models decay — the system must keep learning.

traps.trap: Hard-blocking everything above a low threshold.
traps.why: Blocks many legitimate users (false positives), hurting the business more than the fraud.
traps.instead: Challenge medium risk and route ambiguous cases to review.

flows.name: Tiered outcome: block / challenge / review
flows.note: Confident fraud is blocked inline, medium risk gets a step-up challenge, and ambiguous cases are allowed-but-flagged to an analyst whose label feeds training.

steps.title: 5a. Decision Tiers and Thresholds
description: Not every score should be a hard allow/block — the tiers and their thresholds are a deliberate design. Block only high-confidence fraud (false positives turn away real customers and revenue). For the uncertain middle, issue a step-up challenge (2FA, ID check) inline so a legit user can prove themselves. For genuinely ambiguous cases, allow-but-flag to async review rather than guessing.

Thresholds are tuned against the business cost matrix: the cost of a false positive (blocked good user) vs a false negative (missed fraud) is rarely symmetric, and it differs by action (a $5 charge vs a $5,000 one). Calibrate the score and set per-action thresholds accordingly.

These cutoffs are not static — they're monitored and adjusted as fraud patterns and approval/chargeback rates shift, which is why the feedback loop (next step) matters. Treat the thresholds as a versioned decision_policies record: changes are auditable, rolled out safely (canary a new policy before making it champion, with a rollback), and measured by approval rate, challenge completion rate, fraud loss, false-positive rate, and review-queue load — so a threshold tweak that quietly doubles the analyst backlog is caught. The active policyVersion is returned on every decision and stored on its audit record, so any decision can be replayed against the exact thresholds that produced it (and a bad rollout can be attributed precisely).

view.caption: The risk API routes scores in the uncertain middle band to the review queue rather than hard-blocking them.

steps.decisionPrompt: A charge scores 0.6. Block, challenge, or review? How do you set the cutoffs?

concepts.term: Tiered thresholds on a cost matrix
concepts.definition: Block / challenge / review bands set from the asymmetric cost of false positives vs false negatives, per action.
concepts.whyItMatters: It balances fraud caught against legit users blocked — the core business tradeoff.
concepts.example: score>0.9 block; 0.5-0.9 challenge; flagged -> review; thresholds vary by amount.

traps.trap: One global block threshold for all actions.
traps.why: It over-blocks low-risk actions and under-protects high-value ones; ignores the asymmetric cost.
traps.instead: Per-action, cost-calibrated tiers with a challenge band for the uncertain middle.

recap.after: Calibrated block/challenge/review tiers tuned to per-action costs.
recap.newRisk: Thresholds drift as fraud adapts — they need monitoring and the feedback loop.

steps.title: 6. The Feedback Loop: Labels and Retraining
description: Adversaries evolve, so the system must learn. Confirmed outcomes — chargebacks (fraud), analyst labels, user reports — flow into a label store. These labels train the next model version.

Label lag is the catch: a chargeback may arrive weeks after the charge, so training must align labels to the features as they were at decision time (point-in-time correctness), not current values. The per-decision feature snapshot captures the features/score used, enabling this. Labels also differ in trust: analyst labels are high-confidence but few, chargebacks are authoritative but slow, user reports are noisy — carry a source-trust level so the trainer can weight or quarantine low-trust (and potentially poisoned) labels.

Guard against leakage: train on the resolved label and the decision-time feature snapshot only. Analyst-only investigative evidence (notes, signals an analyst pulled after the fact, anything not available at scoring) must not become a feature — it leaks the future into training and inflates offline metrics that collapse in production. The point-in-time join is precisely what enforces this: it can only see what the feature store served at decision time.

Offline training publishes a versioned model into a registry with its validation metrics, feature-set compatibility, rollout state (shadow -> canary -> champion), and a rollback path. Deploy gradually (shadow/champion-challenger), monitor for drift, and roll back to the prior champion on regression. Rules can be updated instantly for emerging patterns while the model retrains on the slower cadence.

view.caption: Analyst outcomes land in the label store, which feeds the offline trainer that publishes a new ML scoring model.

steps.decisionPrompt: A chargeback confirms fraud 3 weeks after the charge. How do you use it to improve the model correctly?

concepts.definition: Joining late-arriving labels to the feature values as they were at decision time.
concepts.whyItMatters: Avoids training leakage and reflects what the model actually saw.
concepts.example: Chargeback label joined to the action's snapshot features from 3 weeks ago.
concepts.group: The Feedback Loop: Labels and Retraining

concepts.definition: Publish each model with validation metrics + rollout state (shadow/canary/champion) and a rollback path; weight labels by how trustworthy their source is.
concepts.whyItMatters: Safe rollout/rollback prevents a bad model from going wide, and trust weighting blunts noisy or poisoned labels.
concepts.example: m_43 canaried before champion; user-report labels weighted below chargebacks.
concepts.group: The Feedback Loop: Labels and Retraining

whyNow: The feedback loop is what keeps the engine ahead of adversaries; point-in-time labeling is the subtle ML-systems correctness point.

recap.after: Outcomes -> point-in-time labels -> retrained models; rules updated instantly.
recap.newRisk: Inline scoring is on the critical path — it must be fast and fail safe at high volume.

steps.title: 7. Latency, Failure Modes, and Adversarial Robustness
description: Scoring is inline, so guard the latency budget: feature reads from an in-memory store, a bounded model, rules short-circuiting cheap cases, and caching repeated entity lookups. Heavy graph computation runs offline/near-real-time, not inline.

Budget the tail explicitly: p95 ~35ms and p99 ~50ms, sliced as rules ~5ms, feature reads ~10ms, model ~15ms, precomputed graph features ~5ms, overhead ~15ms. Alert on the fallback rate and on feature staleness, not just on errors.

Fail safe is per-action, not one universal 'fail open'. If the model or feature store is slow/down, degrade by action and risk rather than blocking the host action entirely — a risk-engine outage shouldn't take down checkout, but it also shouldn't wave through a $5,000 payment. A fallback matrix: a login can challenge (step-up), a small payment can allow-with-flag, a high-value payment requires step-up or review, account creation rate-limits. Every fallback decision logs its reason (model_timeout / feature_timeout / stale_features) and records that the model/features were missing, so the audit trail is honest.

The audit trail itself needs sizing: ~5k decisions/sec x ~1-2 KB is ~0.5-1 TB/day, retained 12-24 months for disputes and regulators. That's too much to keep hot, so tier it — recent decisions (the dispute/chargeback window, weeks) stay in a queryable hot store indexed by decision_id and entity, and older records compress and roll to cold object storage that's still retrievable for an audit but not on the live query path. The feature_snapshots and labels that training joins against follow the same hot/cold split.

Privacy is per record type, not one blanket rule, because this system holds sensitive linkage data (user-device-IP-card relationships, review evidence, chargebacks). A workable retention/access matrix: decisions retained 12-24 months (dispute/audit) with internal access; raw events tiered and compacted after weeks; graph_edges expire by edge type and confidence (a stale shared-IP edge is a false-positive source); review evidence is restricted to analyst RBAC and never trains the model; labels are kept for the model audit horizon; card/IP identifiers are tokenized or salted everywhere, not stored raw; and attacker-facing reason strings are stored separately from the internal explanations so the obscured customer message can never leak the real rule. Deletion requests are honored against the raw-PII stores while the tokenized, non-identifying decision/audit records are retained for compliance.

Adversarial robustness: keep internal reason codes internal — show the user vague messaging while logging detailed reasons for analysts/disputes, so attackers can't reverse-engineer the rules. Rate-limit and monitor the scoring API for probing, defend the event/label pipeline against poisoning (dedupe, source-trust weighting, drift monitors), and keep rules fast-updatable to respond to new attacks faster than retraining allows.

view.caption: The caller's inline request reaches the risk API, which evaluates rules and calls the model, which reads from the feature store under a latency budget.

steps.decisionPrompt: The ML scoring service times out during checkout. Should checkout fail?
whyNow: Closing on latency, fail-safe behavior, and adversarial concerns makes the engine production-real — it must be fast, robust, and not become a SPOF.

recap.newRisk: Drift and adversary adaptation are continuous — bounded by monitoring, fast rules, and retraining.

bottlenecks.issue: Feature/model latency on the critical path.
bottlenecks.mitigation: In-memory features, bounded model, rule short-circuits, caching; p99 ~50ms budget with per-stage slices.
Per-action fallback matrix (challenge / allow-with-flag / step-up / rate-limit) instead of one universal fail-open; never hard-fail the host action; alert on fallback rate.

bottlenecks.issue: Hot keys (shared IP, popular merchant, attack device).
bottlenecks.mitigation: Partition features/stream by entity and shard hot keys so one entity can't bottleneck a partition.

failureDrills.scenario: The ML model or feature store times out during a high-value payment.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Per the fallback matrix it does NOT silently allow: it requires step-up or routes to review, while a low-value action may allow-with-flag — host action stays up either way.
failureDrills.mitigation: Per-action fallback matrix + conservative default; log fallback reason; alert on fallback rate and feature staleness.

failureDrills.scenario: An attacker probes the scoring API to reverse-engineer rules, or replays events to poison features/labels.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: User-facing messaging stays vague (no internal reason codes leak); probing is rate-limited and alerted; dedupe + source-trust weighting + drift monitors blunt poisoning.
failureDrills.mitigation: Internal-only reason codes, API rate limits, event dedupe, label source-trust, drift/poisoning monitors.

flows.name: Per-action fallback on scoring outage
flows.note: If the model or feature store times out, the risk API degrades per the fallback matrix — rules-only for low-risk, step-up/review for high-value — rather than failing the host action or blindly allowing.

messages.label: decision + fallback reason (host action stays up)

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Real-Time Risk Engine
finalDesign.description: Callers ask the risk API inline with a stable action id + idempotency key so retries replay the original decision; it combines a rules engine (fast, explainable, instantly updatable) with an ML model that scores using fresh features. Every action streams dedup'd events to a near-real-time aggregator that maintains velocity/reputation features (online/offline parity) and an entity link graph for ring detection — graph features are materialized offline, never traversed inline. Decisions are tiered — block confident fraud, challenge medium risk inline, allow-and-flag ambiguous cases to an analyst review queue (a case state machine with priority, SLA, evidence snapshot, and audit trail) whose labels (plus chargebacks, weighted by source trust) train the next model with point-in-time correctness against a per-decision feature snapshot. A challenge or review is observable to the caller: the decision returns a challengeId or reviewCaseId, status is queryable (poll or webhook), and completion flows back as an outcome event that closes the case and emits the label. State ownership is explicit: the event log owns raw events, the feature store owns served features + snapshots, the graph owns entity links, review_cases own review state, and versioned rulesets/decision_policies/model_versions own rollout. Scoring runs within a tight p99 budget and degrades per a per-action fallback matrix (challenge / allow-with-flag / step-up / rate-limit) on model/feature outage — not one universal fail-open — with all decisions audited, identifiers tokenized, and attacker-facing reasons obscured.

view.caption: Callers ask the risk API inline, which blends rules and the model fed by a streaming aggregator's features and entity graph, tiers decisions to a review queue, and loops analyst labels back through the trainer.

nonFunctional.how: In-memory features, bounded model, rule short-circuits.
Decision records with reasons + model version.
Per-action fallback matrix on scoring outage (not universal fail-open).
Tokenized identifiers, internal-only reason codes, API rate-limits, event dedupe, label source-trust, drift/poisoning monitors.

say: Confirm inline real-time risk decisions with tiered outcomes.
Pin low latency, recall-vs-false-positives, and adaptability.
Streaming velocity features in a feature store.
Latency budget, per-action fallback matrix, adversarial concerns.
Tradeoffs: strictness vs conversion, sync vs async, label lag.
Mention drift monitoring and challenger models as extensions.

expectations: Scores with rules and/or a model inline.
May miss velocity features, graph, and feedback loop.
Reasons about point-in-time labeling and label lag.

Designs entity-graph features and a per-action fallback matrix, reasoning about per-action degradation, feature staleness, fallback-rate alerting, and audit records when the model/features are missing.
Discusses false-positive cost, adversarial adaptation, and drift precisely.

followUps: How do you avoid training leakage given that labels (chargebacks) arrive weeks later?
How do you keep false positives low without letting fraud through?
How would you detect and respond to a new attack faster than retraining allows?
How do you build entity-graph features without blowing the latency budget?
How do you measure the engine's effectiveness (and not just block rate)? Think fraud loss, false-positive rate, challenge completion, approval rate, review backlog age, and fallback rate.
Inline callers retry — how do you keep retries from double-counting velocity or creating conflicting decisions?
An attacker replays events or floods the scoring API — how do you defend features/labels from poisoning and resist probing?
When the model or feature store is down on a high-value payment, what should happen — and why isn't 'fail open' the right answer everywhere?
Review arrives at ~50-150 cases/sec but analysts clear far fewer — how do you size the review band, prioritize, and throttle the allow-but-flag tier when the backlog ages?
After a stream outage or a feature-definition change, how do you replay/backfill features without double-counting velocity or corrupting point-in-time training joins?
Shared-IP/device edges go stale and cause false positives — how do you expire edges and invalidate the materialized graph features that were derived from them?
A bad model or policy rollout is hurting approvals right now — what's the emergency rollback path, and how fast can you revert thresholds/rules versus the model?
